dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex from the rescue squad. What caused you to call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing since last night, and it's gotten worse. I also have chest pain and feel like my throat is closing, like an asthma attack.
Can you tell me your name, where you are, and the time?
She's opening her eyes spontaneously and is clearly aware.
Her responses are helpful and she appears attentive.
She's able to move all four extremities.
She is alert, oriented to person, place, time, and event. GCS is 15.
With difficulty breathing, is there any hoarseness, or a high-pitched sound in your throat?
Let me check your lungs again to make sure they are moving air normally.
You're breathing quickly-22 breaths per minute-and the breaths are shallow.
Your breathing looks labored and slightly uneven. Is it hard to take deep breaths?
Yes, it feels tight and constricted.
Left lung is clear. The right upper is clear, but the right lower is diminished.
Blood oxygen is at 89%. We'll give you more oxygen now.
Checking pulse and blood pressure now.
Heart rate is very high, at 123 beats per minute and feels strong.
Blood pressure is 211 over 148-elevated but stable right now.
Skin is pink, dry, and warm. No cyanosis or changes in color.
You mentioned chest pain. How long has it lasted?
It started with the breathing issues and has been getting a bit worse.
What makes the chest pain worse?
It feels worse when I take deep breaths.
How would you describe this chest pain?
It aches like pressure over the heart area, feels heavy.
Does this pain move anywhere?
No, it stays right in the center of my chest.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how bad is the chest pain now?
It's about a 4.
When exactly did this chest pain start becoming more noticeable?
Around 30 minutes ago.
Do you have any medical conditions I should be aware of?
I have end-stage kidney disease and depend on dialysis. I also have GERD, high blood pressure, and schizoaffective disorder.
You are currently taking amlodipine, carvedilol, furosemide, and fluticasone. You have known allergies to Zyprexa, clonidine, Lopressor, Phoslo, and candesartan.
Do you use an MDI, and have you used it today?
I have one, but I couldn't get down the stairs to the MDI and haven't used it.
Can you cough, and are you able to bring up any sputum?
I can cough, but no phlegm or sputum.
Skin still appears warm, dry, and pink. No scars or rashes to note.
Moving on to the head and neck. You show no signs of neck swelling or stiffness.
You are breathing from the mouth; no signs of nasal blockage or discharge from the nose.
Your left lung remains clear, and right lung now shows movement with adequate volume.
Looking at your abdomen. Nothing is tender, rigid, or swollen.
No deformity or pulses missing, and all extremities appear intact.
Double-checking pulse-123 beats per minute-still high.
Your breathing rate is 20 and appears a little easier now.
Rechecking blood pressure-now at 208 over 150.
A first glucose reading was 84, second attempt was inconclusive.
Oxygen saturation has improved-now at 90%.
We're now placing a 4-lead ECG to monitor for any changes.
Administering a nebulizer treatment with Duoneb at 6 liters of oxygen per minute.
Recheck vitals-including repeating readings after the medication is administered.
Breathing rate has lowered slightly to 18 and more even.
Oxygen levels improved significantly-now at 100%.
Your second glucose reading failed due to ongoing nebulizer use.
Performing a 12-lead ECG now for further evaluation.
We are transporting you to Henrico Doctors' Forest Hospital.
You'll ride seated for comfort and safety.
You are now loaded into the ambulance. Is that comfortable?
The ride will be without lights and sirens-conditions are not critical.
We will now load you into the ambulance safely with RFD assistance.
You remain monitored and quietly breathing during the transport.
Recheck before movement-208/145, 121, 18, and oxygen at 100%.
We are now finishing the nebulizer treatment, you were on 6 liters of oxygen via mask.
Your vitals remain stable and your breathing is controlled.
We are already en route, and driving to the hospital without emergency lights.
Thanos, we're calling the hospital now with a full patient transfer report.
The patient received oxygen and a nebulizer treatment. A 4-lead and 12-lead ECG were completed.
Eleven hundred forty-five 12-lead ECG showed sinus tach with minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, and left atrial enlargement.
At the hospital, we'll transfer you from the cot to the bed using a sheet pull method with two staff.
First blood sugar was 84, second attempt failed because of continuous treatment.
Full patient report was given to the nurse. Care was transferred smoothly.
Hospital staff accepted the patient and the transfer was completed.
Patient signed admission forms for the hospital.
Nurse finalized and signed all transfer documents.
